question: Your service reports 92% line coverage and the CI gate is set at 90%. A reviewer asks what that number tells you about the quality of the test suite. What is the most accurate answer?
options: That roughly 92% of the code's behaviour has been verified, so the remaining risk is concentrated in the 8% of lines nothing touches

Almost nothing on its own. Measured here: six tests that call every function and contain zero assertions scored 100.0% line coverage (48 of 48 lines) and 87.5% branch coverage while killing 0 of 70 seeded faults. Coverage and detection are independent quantities
That the suite is adequate for the covered code but says nothing about integration behaviour, which is what the remaining 8% represents
That the suite is probably fine — 92% is above the industry-standard 80% threshold, and pushing higher has diminishing returns because the last lines are error handlers
explanation: The tempting answer treats coverage as a proxy for verification, which is exactly the belief the metric's name encourages. Coverage records which lines the interpreter executed; it has no access to whether anything compared a result to an expectation. The proof is constructive rather than statistical: a suite wrapping every call in try/except with no assertions reaches the metric's ceiling and detects nothing at all, so a suite can sit anywhere on the range from 0% to 100% detection at the same coverage number. Option 3 is a real concern but a different one — coverage's blindness here has nothing to do with integration. Option 4's 'industry-standard 80%' has no measured basis in anything.

question: A six-line shipping function is exercised by exactly one test, `shipping_cents(6000, True)`. Both of its `if` statements come out true. What do line and branch coverage report?
options: Line 100.0% and branch 100.0% — every statement ran and every decision was evaluated, which is what branch coverage measures
Line 66.7% and branch 50.0% — the two statements inside the untaken else paths did not run

Line 100.0% (6 of 6) and branch 50.0% (2 of 4). Both `if` statements have no `else`, so there is no statement on the false side to leave unexecuted — but each decision contributes two outcomes and only the true one was taken
Line 100.0% and branch 75.0% — the second `if` is nested under the first, so its false outcome is only partially reachable
explanation: The trap is thinking branch coverage counts decisions *evaluated* rather than outcomes *taken*. A decision contributes two units and you need both. The deeper point is structural: an `if` with no `else` costs you nothing in line coverage, because the false side contains no statement to miss. Backend code is overwhelmingly made of guard clauses, early returns, null checks and feature flags — all `if` with no `else` — so line coverage is systematically blindest exactly where backend code is densest. That is the argument for turning on `--branch`, which is off by default in coverage.py.

question: Five suites of increasing quality are measured against the same 48-line module and the same 70 seeded faults. Suites 3, 4 and 5 all report 100.0% line coverage and 100.0% branch coverage. What are their mutation scores?
options: All three are close together — once both coverage metrics are maxed out, a suite has exercised every statement and every decision, so the remaining differences are small

78.6%, 88.6% and 95.7%. The 13 tests separating suite 3 from suite 5 are worth 17 points of detection and exactly zero points of coverage, because they add assertions and boundary cases rather than reaching new code
They fall as the suites grow, because adding more tests over the same code increases the chance that one of them is a tautology that dilutes the score
The scores cannot be compared — mutation score depends on which operators are enabled, so three suites measured against the same operator set will still produce incomparable numbers
explanation: The first option is the natural inference and it is exactly backwards: coverage saturating is the *start* of the interesting range, not the end of it. Line coverage here was already at its maximum on suite 1 — the no-assert suite — and branch coverage was finished by suite 3, while the mutation score had not stopped climbing at suite 5. Option 4 states something true in general (mutation scores are not comparable across codebases or operator sets) and then draws the wrong conclusion: comparing two suites over the *same* code with the *same* operators is precisely the comparison the metric is valid for, and it is the one that matters when you are deciding whether a test was worth writing.

question: After running 70 mutants against the strongest suite, 3 survive. Every one of them is indistinguishable from the original across all 302 probe calls. What should you conclude and do?
options: They are equivalent mutants — the probe set has proved it. Record the ceiling as 95.7%, exclude those three from future runs, and move on
The probe set is too small. Increase it until each survivor is separated, since any two syntactically different programs must differ on some input

They are equivalence candidates, not proven equivalents — a probe can prove a mutant killable but never prove one equivalent. Read all three by hand; here each turned out genuinely equivalent, putting the achievable ceiling at 95.7% rather than 100%
The suite has three gaps. Write a test for each survivor, since a surviving mutant is by definition a fault the suite failed to detect
explanation: Option 1 gets the outcome right and the epistemics wrong, which matters because the same reasoning applied to a larger mutant set will mislead you. Agreement across 302 probes is evidence, not a theorem; deciding equivalence in general reduces to program equivalence and is undecidable (Jia & Harman, IEEE TSE 37(5), 2011). Option 2 is false — two programs can be syntactically different and compute the same function on every input, which is exactly what an equivalent mutant is. Option 4 is the default assumption and it is why teams abandon mutation testing: chasing an unkillable survivor is unbounded work. The correct workflow is the triage measured here — against a weaker suite, 15 survived and 12 were separated by some probe, each of those 12 arriving with a concrete failing input already attached.

question: The correct reading of coverage rests on an asymmetry between two conditional probabilities. Which pair is right, and what follows from it?
options: P(fault detected | line executed) is high and P(fault detected | line never executed) is low, so coverage is a good but noisy proxy for detection and should be gated at a high threshold

P(fault detected | line never executed) = 0 exactly and universally, while P(fault detected | line executed) measured 84.6% and is never 1. So coverage is a ceiling: low coverage is hard evidence of a gap, high coverage is evidence of nothing
Both probabilities are empirical and codebase-specific, so the only sound use of coverage is tracking its trend over time rather than any absolute level
P(fault detected | line executed) = 1 by definition, since executing mutated code necessarily produces different behaviour that some assertion will catch
explanation: The asymmetry is the whole point and it is almost never stated precisely. One side is a theorem: a test cannot observe a difference in code that never runs during it, so an uncovered line is a guaranteed hole in every program, language and suite. The other side is merely empirical and well below 1 — executing a line buys the *possibility* of detection and nothing more. Option 4 conflates infection with propagation: mutated code can execute, produce a different intermediate value, and still return the same answer, or return a different answer that nothing asserts on. The asymmetry also pays practically: because unexecuted lines are guaranteed survivors, `mutmut --use-coverage` skips their mutants with no loss of information at all.

question: You want mutation testing in CI without an 18-hour job. The measured mutant density is 1.46 per executable line and your suite takes 30 seconds. What configuration follows?
options: Run the full-repo mutation on every pull request but cap the mutant count with a random sample, so each PR gets an unbiased estimate of the whole repository's score
Keep the total-coverage gate at 90% for pull requests and add mutation testing quarterly, since mutation is too slow to be part of any regular process

Gate on changed-line coverage at 100% with diff-cover, run mutation on the diff in the PR — a 60-line diff is about 88 mutants and 5.5 minutes on 8 workers — and run --paths-to-mutate over the high-consequence modules nightly, where 1,167 mutants is about 1.2 hours
Gate on mutation score for the whole repository at 100%, run nightly, and quarantine the modules that fail until someone fixes them
explanation: The cost model is `mutants × suite runtime` and mutants scale with lines, so the only variable you control is how many lines you point it at — which is what makes the diff the right unit for a blocking gate. Option 1's random sample sounds statistically sensible and answers the wrong question: you do not need an estimate of your repository's overall score in a PR, you need to know whether *this change* is tested. Option 4 sets a target that is provably unreachable — the measured ceiling here was 95.7% because of equivalent mutants, and a 100% threshold will be met by deleting inconvenient mutants rather than by writing tests. Note also what the recommendation removes: the total-coverage gate is deleted rather than lowered, because any named number becomes a target.
